preFix.title: Polymer clay faux labradorite pendant
preFix.subtitle: A layered polymer stone pendant with metallic blue-green flash, sanded and buffed to high shine
preFix.excerpt: This pendant mimics the characteristic metallic flash of labradorite by layering dark grey and charcoal polymer clay with pearl-tone clay to align metallic particles in strata. The finished pendant is approximately 40 mm by 30 mm and 4 mm thick. Cured in a domestic oven, sanded through to 1000 grit, and buffed to a stone-like shine.

content.text: This pendant reproduces the
labradorescence

of natural labradorite by stacking layers of dark grey, charcoal, and pearl-tone polymer clay. Folding and compressing those layers aligns the metallic particles in strata so that, when the pendant is sliced and sanded, angled light picks out a blue-green flash across the surface. No kiln is required: polymer clay hardens in a domestic oven at 110 to 130 degrees C.

tools.name: Wooden rolling pin or acrylic roller

materials.name: Polymer clay, pearl or metallic blue-green

content.text: Preparation

Work on a smooth non-porous surface such as a ceramic tile or a sheet of baking parchment on a wooden board. Warm the clay by kneading it for a minute or two until it is supple and holds its shape without cracking at the edges. Keep your hands clean between colours: even a small amount of dark clay transferred to pearl clay will muddy the metallic flash. Wet-dry abrasive paper in 400, 600, 800, and 1000 grit is needed for finishing, as are a jump ring and jewellery wire for hanging. Pre-heat the oven before placing the pendant inside.
Construction

Roll the dark grey clay into a slab approximately 3 mm thick and 80 mm by 60 mm. Roll the charcoal clay into a thinner slab of approximately 1.5 mm thickness and the same footprint. Roll the pearl or metallic clay into a very thin sheet, approximately 0.5 mm thick.

Stack the layers: grey at the base, pearl in the middle, charcoal on top. Press gently with flat hands to consolidate the stack without distorting the layers. The total thickness should be approximately 5 mm.
Fold the stack in half from left to right so the layers double up. Roll gently to 5 mm again. Repeat this fold-and-roll cycle four more times, always folding in the same direction. This mimics the
Skinner blend
family of techniques and aligns the metallic particles into parallel strata.

After five fold-and-roll cycles, compress the block to approximately 6 mm thickness. Slice off a slab approximately 4 mm thick from one end using the fettling knife in a single clean cut. The exposed face shows the strata.

Cut the pendant shape from the slice: approximately 40 mm long by 30 mm wide. An elongated oval or irregular stone shape is more convincing than a rectangle. Use the fettling knife in one curved pass, or cut to a template drawn on paper.

Pierce the pendant with the needle tool approximately 3 mm from the top, running the needle through the full 4 mm thickness to create the jump ring hole. Rotate the needle gently rather than pushing it straight through to avoid cracking the clay.
Place the pendant on a tile or folded card and transfer to the oven.
Cure
at 110 to 130 degrees C (check the manufacturer guidance on your specific clay) for 30 minutes. Do not exceed the recommended temperature or the surface will scorch.
Curing

After 30 minutes remove the pendant from the oven and allow it to cool completely on the tile before handling. The clay is soft and flexible immediately after removal and hardens fully as it cools. Do not quench in water. Once cool, the pendant should be firm and rigid with no flex.
Finishing

Sand the pendant through wet-dry abrasive paper in sequence: 400 grit, then 600, then 800, then 1000. Use water as a lubricant with each grit. The surface will appear dull and uniformly scratched at each stage, which is correct. After 1000 grit the surface should feel glass-smooth. Buff with a soft cloth at high speed, or apply two thin coats of satin or gloss varnish, allowing full drying between coats. Attach a jump ring through the pierced hole and add a chain or cord.
Care

This pendant is not food-safe and is for decorative wear only. Do not place in a microwave or dishwasher. Wipe clean with a soft dry cloth. Avoid prolonged exposure to direct sunlight, which can cause polymer clay to become brittle over years. Store flat or hanging to avoid flexing the jump ring connection.
Variations
Gold flash version: substitute a gold or bronze metallic sheet layer for the blue-green pearl to approximate golden labradorite.
Faux tiger's eye: use amber and dark brown layers with a metallic copper or gold mid-sheet, fold fewer times to keep the bands wide.
Matched earring set: cut two smaller oval slices from the same layered block to produce a pendant-and-earring set with consistent patterning.
Ring top: cut a 20 mm circle and mount on a flat ring blank with jewellery adhesive after sanding.

rows.fix: Increase the fold count to eight or ten, and use a thicker pearl layer (1 mm) next time. More fold cycles concentrate and align the particles more effectively
rows.cause: Insufficient fold cycles, or the metallic layer was too thin to leave visible particles
rows.problem: No visible flash when the pendant is tilted in light

rows.fix: Roll gently and fold only in one consistent direction. Stop folding when you can still see distinct layers in cross section
rows.cause: Over-blending or rolling too aggressively, which disperses rather than aligns the particles
rows.problem: Colours mixing into grey mud rather than showing distinct strata

rows.fix: Bake on the middle rack, use an oven thermometer to verify temperature, and tent the piece loosely with foil to prevent direct radiant heat
rows.cause: Oven temperature too high or clay placed too close to the heating element
rows.problem: Surface scorching or browning after curing

rows.fix: Pierce at least 4 mm from the top edge. If cracked, fill with a small plug of raw clay and re-cure, or reinforce with a drop of cyanoacrylate adhesive
rows.cause: Hole placed too close to the edge, or clay was not fully warm before piercing
rows.problem: Jump ring hole cracking after curing
